target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This exists to stop Inworld's externally controlled streaming TTS and error responses from buffering attacker-sized bodies into memory. The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an unbounded `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting newline-delimited JSON and concatenating base64 audio chunks, so a controlled or hijacked endpoint could stream an arbitrary body and force OpenClaw to buffer it. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`) and the analogous `listInworldVoices` error path had the same unbounded-read shape.

## Changes

- Bound the main `inworldTTS` audio response with `readResponseWithLimit`, using `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) plus a 30s idle timeout.
- Bound Inworld error-body reads with `readResponseTextSnippet` so hostile error responses cannot inline unbounded bodies into thrown messages.
- Re-exported `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so extensions use the existing response-limit entry instead of a new helper path.
- Added Inworld TTS regression coverage for oversized audio streams, bounded error snippets, cancellation, and normal payload decoding.

## Evidence

The earlier proof for this PR used an in-process stream fixture, so this proof was upgraded to a real terminal `node:http` check. The scratchpad proof script is local only and is not committed. It starts a real loopback CONNECT proxy because Inworld's production SSRF policy allowlists the configured hostname and does not permit a direct loopback base URL; the exported `inworldTTS` still drives the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` / undici dispatcher path, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, and a real TCP socket. No in-process `ReadableStream` fixture is used.

Command: `node --import tsx scratchpad/inworld-http-proof.mts`

```text
[proof] real node:http loopback proxy on http://127.0.0.1:33889, target=http://example.com, audioCap=33554432 bytes, audioWouldStream=134217728 bytes
PASS  oversized Inworld TTS audio stream throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 33619968 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)"
PASS  audio stream cancelled: proxy saw socket abort before full body :: aborted=true bytesSent=35651584 (<134217728) chunks=34
PASS  audio stream cancelled near the 32 MiB cap :: bytesSent=35651584 (<67108864)
PASS  negative control: raw unbounded read buffers the full body past the 32 MiB cap :: buffered=134217728 bytes (> 33554432)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded read :: unbounded buffered=134217728 vs bounded sent≈35651584
PASS  oversized error body stays bounded and cancels stream :: messageLength=430 aborted=true bytesSent=1048576 (<33554432) cap=8192
PASS  happy path: small NDJSON audio payload still parses :: audio="real-audio-bytes" bytes=16

[proof] 8 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused regression test:

Command: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
Result      [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.04s
```

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages error-response read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS main audio stream plus its error bodies, reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper through the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry rather than introducing a new abstraction.

**Label**: security

---

target.title: feat(channels): expose source actor attribution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Group-channel messages need a generic way to distinguish the runtime message author from the operator/user represented by the agent session. Without source actor attribution, downstream prompts, tools, and memory adapters can only see channel/session context and may conflate another group participant with the operator. This should be solved as a channel contract, not as a deployment-specific memory backend workaround.

## Summary

- Adds a generic `SourceActor` inbound context contract for the runtime identity of the message author.
- Maps Signal sender identity into `SourceActor` without making the contract Signal-specific.
- Documents that channel plugins should provide source actor attribution generically rather than hard-coding deployment-specific people or memory backends.

## Stack

This is stacked after #96738 and `codex/signal-mention-facts-context`. Until those land, this PR's diff will include its dependencies.

## Production impact

No behavior change by itself. The change gives prompts, tools, and memory integrations a stable way to distinguish a group participant from the operator/user.

## Evidence

- Channel context tests cover generic `SourceActor` projection.
- Signal mention-gating tests cover Signal sender identity mapping into the generic context.
- Broader affected-context regression suite passed:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- Production TypeScript check passed:
  `pnpm tsgo:prod`

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`

target.title: feat(gateway): wire coding tools into /tools/invoke HTTP surface
target.body:
> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `4fe6f873c9` (scope owner gate to built-in host-FS write tools; restacked on #85664 `d625c6de23`).** Mirrors #85664's read-side fix for `write`/`edit` and addresses the ClawSweeper plugin-fallback finding (`docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` write section). Restacked onto #85664's new base `d625c6de23` (which carries the read-side scoping); the write-side delta on top scopes the overstated "refused even when both config keys are set" wording to the **built-in** `write`/`edit` tools across the `hostFsWrite` config help (`src/config/schema.help.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` member comment (`src/config/types.gateway.ts`), the `hostFsWrite` bullet in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, and the write section of `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` (prose, truth table, _Plugin name collision_ note). Per the resolver (`src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`): for a non-owner caller the built-in is never materialized, but a same-named allowlisted plugin tool still resolves under the normal `gateway.tools.allow`/`deny` policy. Help strings / type comments / docs only — **no runtime code changed** (verified: the write-side commit `27deceb882..4fe6f873c9` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/types.gateway.ts`, and the two `docs/gateway/*.md` files — no `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, or `schema.labels.ts`), so the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `pnpm check:docs` (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links) and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `61e6f29bed` (config-help owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]/[P3]).** Restacked onto #85664's new base `7f9876c2b2`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `661d6838c0` is **config help / type docs** (verified: `git diff 661d6838c0 61e6f29bed` touches only `src/config/schema.help.ts` +4 and `src/config/types.gateway.ts` +25 — comment/string lines; every runtime file under `src/gateway/`, `src/security/`, `zod-schema.ts`, `schema.labels.ts` is byte-identical): the `hostFsWrite` `FIELD_HELP` string and the `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary + `hostFsWrite` member comment now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces, and the type summary is refreshed so it no longer calls `hostFsWrite` "future work". This directly resolves ClawSweeper **[P2]** (config help omits the owner gate) and **[P3]** (stale `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` summary). Because no runtime code changed, the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. Verified in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container: `schema.help.quality.test.ts` (23/23) and `pnpm check:docs` both green.

---

> **Update 2026-06-17 — head `661d6838c0` (docs owner-gate fix; resolves [P2]).** Restacked onto #85664's docs-updated read base `ca46eb9946`. The only delta vs the last-reviewed head `afe36ccf45` is **docs**: the `read` + `write`/`edit` truth tables in `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md` and the `hostFsRead`/`hostFsWrite` bullets in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` now document the **owner/admin sender** gate (`senderIsOwner === true`) the resolver enforces — directly resolving ClawSweeper's **[P2]** ("Document the owner gate for file-tool opt-ins"). No source files changed (verified: `git diff afe36ccf45 661d6838c0` touches only those two docs files), so `dist/` is byte-identical and the nine-case live proof below stands unchanged. `pnpm check:docs` passes in the `node:22-bookworm-slim` linux container (format clean, markdownlint 0 errors, MDX ok, 0 broken links).

---

Builds on #85664. Narrow extension of the broader direct-invoke umbrella tracked in #37131.

## Summary

Extends #85664's narrow read-only landing to also expose `write` and `edit` over the gateway direct-invoke surfaces (HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` AND SDK RPC `tools.invoke`, which share the `resolveGatewayScopedTools` resolver). Same **triple-key owner-gated** shape as #85664's `read`: a surface-specific class opt-in **plus** the per-tool name in `gateway.tools.allow` **plus** `senderIsOwner === true`, all on the `http` surface.

## Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base (head `afe36ccf456f`, base upstream `023ce6e96c38`)

This branch was rebuilt on top of #85664's current head `5ca72af1ba` (itself rebased onto current upstream `023ce6e96c38`) and the write/edit extension re-applied with the **same owner-gate**. This directly resolves the prior ClawSweeper [P1].

**Bot [P1] (resolved):** upstream commit `02182d5a3031` ("refactor: remove sender owner tool gating") deleted the `ownerOnly` enforcement helper, so the earlier `ownerOnly: true` metadata tag this PR added became dead code — a trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller could have reached an allowlisted mutating tool with no owner check. **Fix:** this PR no longer relies on that tag. `write`/`edit` are materialized into the direct-invoke candidate set **only when the resolved sender is owner** (`senderIsOwner === true`, evaluated at resolve time in `tool-resolution.ts`), exactly mirroring #85664's read gate. A trusted-proxy `operator.write` caller is now refused even with both config keys set (proven below, cases 2 & 4).

## Triple-key gating

To reach a write-class tool on the direct-invoke surface, ALL must hold:

| Key | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `gateway.tools.directInvoke.hostFsWrite: true` | Class-level opt-in for the whole write family. | `false` |
| `gateway.tools.allow: [...]` includes the tool name | Per-tool enablement. | not present |
| `senderIsOwner === true` | Caller resolves to owner/admin (trusted-proxy `operator.admin`, or shared-secret bearer). | — |

Truth table (tool name `T` ∈ `{write, edit}`, surface = `http`):

| `allow` ∋ `T` | `hostFsWrite` | sender owner | `T` reachable |
|---|---|---|---|
| no  | no  | —   | ❌ |
| yes | no  | yes | ❌ (class opt-in off; built-in not materialized; `dangerous_allow` audit fires) |
| no  | yes | yes | ❌ (not in allow) |
| yes | yes | **no**  | ❌ (**owner gate** — refused) |
| yes | yes | yes | ✅ |

## Scope

- **Adds:** `write`, `edit` to direct-invoke (behind `hostFsWrite: true` + allow + owner).
- **Inherits from #85664:** `read` via `hostFsRead: true` + allow + owner.
- **`apply_patch`:** present in `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY` for defense-in-depth, but the coding-tools factory produces no `apply_patch` entry, so it is never materialized (proven below, case 7).
- **NOT exposed:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` — RCE-class; out of scope for this PR.

## Audit

- `gateway.tools_invoke_http.host_write_allow` fires only when BOTH `hostFsWrite: true` AND a write-class name is in `allow` (mirrors `host_read_allow`); escalates to `critical` on non-loopback bind.
- `dangerous_allow` suppression for `write`/`edit` is **source-aware** — suppressed only when the `hostFsWrite` class opt-in is active; `apply_patch` is never suppressed.

## Files changed

- `src/config/types.gateway.ts` — adds `hostFsWrite?: boolean` to `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns`
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — `hostFsWrite` schema entry
- `src/config/schema.help.ts`, `src/config/schema.labels.ts` — field help + label
- `src/security/dangerous-tools.ts` — adds `"write"`, `"edit"` to `DEFAULT_GATEWAY_HTTP_TOOL_DENY`
- `src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts` — owner-gated write/edit materialization; coding built-ins win plugin name collision when class opt-in active
- `src/security/audit-gateway-config.ts` — `host_write_allow` finding; source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression
- `src/gateway/tools-invoke-http.test.ts` — write coding-tool collision precedence (owner→built-in, non-owner→plugin, opt-in-off→plugin)
- `src/security/audit-gateway-tools-http.test.ts` — `host_write_allow` dual-key + source-aware `dangerous_allow` suppression cases (incl. `apply_patch` never suppressed)
- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts` — coding-tool factory plumbing
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md`, `docs/gateway/tools-invoke-http-api.md`, `docs/gateway/security/audit-checks.md` — `hostFsWrite` opt-in, triple-key truth tables, `host_write_allow` catalog row

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Extend #85664's owner-gated direct-invoke `read` to also expose `write` and `edit` over the Gateway HTTP `POST /tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke` surfaces, behind the same triple key (class opt-in `directInvoke.hostFsWrite` + per-tool `gateway.tools.allow` + `senderIsOwner`). Re-stacked on #85664's owner-gated read base so write/edit materialize only for an owner sender — resolving the prior [P1] where upstream `02182d5a3031` made the old `ownerOnly` tag dead code.

**Real environment tested:** Built fresh from head `afe36ccf456f` inside a `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64-native container (full runtime build → `dist/index.js`; SDK → `packages/sdk/dist/index.mjs`). Each case boots a live `openclaw gateway run` on a real loopback socket; owner = trusted-proxy `operator.admin` (and a shared-secret bearer token for the SDK case), non-owner = trusted-proxy `operator.write`. Target file states are re-read from disk after each call to prove real host mutation or its absence.

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:** `PROOF_HEAD=afe36ccf456f bash .proof-63919.sh` inside the container — boots nine gateway configs and dispatches `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` over HTTP `/tools/invoke` and SDK `tools.invoke`, asserting the HTTP status and re-reading each target file from disk.

**Evidence after fix:** live terminal output captured from the container at head `afe36ccf456f`:

```
=== HEAD: afe36ccf456f ===

### LIVE PROOF (HTTP trusted-proxy surface) ###

--- 1 W-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 24 bytes to /work/.proof63/w1.txt"}]}}
  file=OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f
--- 2 W-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=PRESEED
--- 3 E-OWNER-ENABLED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in /work/.proof63/e3.txt."}],"details":{"diff":" 1 ALPHA\\n-2 BETA\\n+2 GAMMA-afe36ccf456f", ...}}}
  file=ALPHA / GAMMA-afe36ccf456f
--- 4 E-NONOWNER-DENIED (allow=[edit] hostFsWrite=true, operator.write, tool=edit) ---
  [HTTP operator.write] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: edit"}}
  file=ALPHA / BETA  (unchanged)
--- 5 W-DENIED-no-opt-in (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=false, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 6 W-DENIED-no-allow (allow=[] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=write) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: write"}}
  file=<absent>
--- 7 APPLYPATCH-denied (allow=[apply_patch] hostFsWrite=true, operator.admin, tool=apply_patch) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 404 {"ok":false,"error":{"type":"not_found","message":"Tool not available: apply_patch"}}
--- 8 READ-REGRESSION (allow=[read] hostFsRead=true, operator.admin, tool=read) ---
  [HTTP operator.admin] 200 {"ok":true,"result":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"READ-BASE-OK-afe36ccf456f\\n"}]}}

### LIVE PROOF (SDK token surface — shared-secret bearer = owner) ###

--- 9 SDK-W-OWNER (allow=[write] hostFsWrite=true, token bearer, tool=write) ---
  [SDK token] {"ok":true,"toolName":"write","output":{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Successfully wrote 28 bytes to /work/.proof63/w9.txt"}]},"source":"core"}
  file=SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f

=== PROOF COMPLETE: HEAD=afe36ccf456f ===
```

Independent re-read of persisted file states after the run: `w1.txt`=`OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`, `w2.txt`=`PRESEED` (non-owner write left it untouched), `e3.txt`=`ALPHA/GAMMA-afe36ccf456f`, `e4.txt`=`ALPHA/BETA` (non-owner edit left it untouched), `w5.txt` and `w6.txt` absent (never created), `w9.txt`=`SDK-OWNER-WROTE-afe36ccf456f`.

**Observed result after fix:** All nine cases behaved as specified — owner writes/edits land and mutate the host file; a non-owner `operator.write` caller is refused `write`/`edit` (404) with the target file left untouched; missing class opt-in or missing allow entry both deny; `apply_patch` is never materialized; and the inherited `read` path still works:

| # | Case | Config | Caller | HTTP/SDK | File state |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | write owner | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 wrote 24 bytes | written |
| 2 | write **non-owner** | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 3 | edit owner | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 200 replaced 1 block | edited |
| 4 | edit **non-owner** | allow=[edit], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.write` | 404 not_found | **unchanged** (owner gate) |
| 5 | write no opt-in | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=false | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 6 | write no allow | allow=[], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | never created |
| 7 | apply_patch | allow=[apply_patch], hostFsWrite=true | `operator.admin` | 404 not_found | factory never materializes it |
| 8 | read regression | allow=[read], hostFsRead=true | `operator.admin` | 200 + contents | #85664 base intact |
| 9 | write owner (SDK) | allow=[write], hostFsWrite=true | token bearer | ok=true wrote 28 bytes | written |

Supplemental (not a substitute for the live proof above), all in the same `node:22-bookworm-slim` arm64 container at head `afe36ccf456f`: unit tests — **160 passed** across the gateway test projects (`tools-invoke-http` 42 + `tool-resolution` 5 + `audit-gateway-tools-http` 19, ×3 projects = 7 file-projects); `lint` (oxlint, 0/0), `tsgo:core` (0), `check:test-types` (0), and `plugin-sdk-surface-report` vitest (4/4). **`checks-node-core-fast` / plugin-SDK surface budget:** this PR adds `hostFsWrite?` as a *field* on the existing `GatewayDirectInvokeOptIns` type — **zero new top-level exports** — so `plugin-sdk-surface-report --check` passes on the `+1` budget already carried by #85664's base commit (`config-types` 416 / public 10285 / public-fn 5164 / deprecated 3238). No further budget change in this PR.

**Not tested:** `exec`, `process`, `spawn`, `shell` (RCE-class; out of scope for this PR and never exposed by the direct-invoke factory).

target.title: fix(signal): expose mention facts in inbound context
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Signal group mention gating now has native mention metadata available, but the decision is only visible inside the Signal monitor. Downstream prompt/tool context cannot inspect whether the inbound group message had native mention metadata, whether `requireMention` was enforced, or why a message was skipped. That makes native-only group behavior hard to debug and forces consumers to infer channel-specific facts from text.

## Summary

- Exposes Signal mention-gating facts on the generic inbound message context.
- Adds `CanDetectMention`, `HasAnyMention`, `RequireMention`, `EffectiveWasMentioned`, `MentionShouldSkip`, and `MentionSource` for native/text mention decisions.
- Keeps the Signal mention source literal typed so SDK/context consumers can rely on `"native"` vs `"mention_pattern"`.

## Stack

This is stacked after #96738 (`fix(signal): detect native bot mentions in group gating`). Until #96738 lands, this PR's diff will include that dependency.

## Production impact

No new outbound behavior. This only makes already-computed mention gating facts inspectable by prompts, tests, and plugin/channel contracts.

## Evidence

- Focused Signal/channel tests cover native mention facts being projected into inbound context:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- Broader affected-context regression suite passed:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- Production TypeScript check passed:
  `pnpm tsgo:prod`

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts extensions/memory-core/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts src/channels/inbound-event/context.test.ts extensions/signal/src/monitor/event-handler.mention-gating.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`

target.title: feat(i18n): localize runtime user-facing copy
target.body:
## Summary

Localizes shared runtime/user-facing copy through the existing wizard i18n runtime translator and adds English, Simplified Chinese, and Traditional Chinese locale entries for the new runtime keys.

## What changed

- Added runtime copy keys under `runtime.channel`, `runtime.gateway`, and `runtime.tui` in the wizard i18n locale maps.
- Routed shared agent/runtime reply copy through `runtimeT(...)`, including rate limits, billing/capacity/overload errors, missing API keys, model login/switch failures, context overflow/compaction notices, fallback backend notices, gateway restart notices, memory flush failures, and LLM socket error formatting.
- Localized gateway-facing reason strings for attachment availability, auth rate limiting, active-session reset/delete conflicts, and webchat media fallback display text.
- Localized TUI thinking labels and session reset/new-session acknowledgements that are shared across runtime surfaces.
- Preserved stable non-display behavior where other code consumes these strings: non-direct silent-reply routing now uses an explicit runner-failure flag, and iMessage, active-memory, and QA-lab classifiers recognize the localized runtime failure text they intentionally inspect.
- Kept English defaults equivalent to the previous user-facing strings so existing behavior remains stable when `OPENCLAW_LOCALE` is unset or unsupported.

## Boundary note

This PR originally scoped the implementation to core `src/**` runtime copy. During review, we found downstream consumers outside `src` that rely on static English user-visible runtime failure text as classification signals rather than stable metadata. Specifically, iMessage reflection detection, active-memory query cleanup, and QA-lab failure detection matched English runtime failure strings.

That is why the current PR includes narrow `extensions/` classifier updates: they preserve existing behavior after core failure copy is localized. The PR does not migrate or rewrite plugin-owned product copy beyond those guards.

## What this intentionally does not do

- Does not migrate every command-specific response yet. `/config`, `/plugins`, `/session`, ACP, TTS, subagents, allowlist, export, diagnostics, and similar command help/status strings still have many command-owned literals.
- Does not change plugin-owned product copy outside the classifier updates needed for localized shared runtime failure strings.
- Does not introduce a new runtime i18n package or move the translator out of `src/wizard/i18n`; this follows the branch's existing runtime translator pattern.
- Does not change protocol codes, gateway error `type` values, or behavioral branching contracts; user-visible text is translated while routing/classification decisions stay on stable metadata or explicit localized classifier contracts.
- Does not include an agent transcript section in this PR body.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Shared runtime and gateway user-visible strings now resolve through locale-aware i18n keys for English, Simplified Chinese, and Traditional Chinese, while localized failure text still preserves existing non-direct suppression and downstream failure classifiers.

Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on macOS after rebasing this branch onto latest `upstream/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'NODE'
import { buildKnownAgentRunFailureReplyPayload, buildPreflightCompactionFailureText } from './src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts';
import { t } from './src/wizard/i18n/index.ts';
for (const locale of ['en', 'zh-CN', 'zh-TW']) {
  process.env.OPENCLAW_LOCALE = locale;
  const missingApiKey = buildKnownAgentRunFailureReplyPayload({
    err: new Error('No API key found for provider "openai". You are authenticated with OpenAI Codex OAuth.'),
    sessionCtx: { Provider: 'whatsapp', MessageSid: 'proof' },
    resolvedVerboseLevel: 'off',
  });
  console.log(`locale=${locale}`);
  console.log(`generic=${t('runtime.channel.genericExternalRunFailure')}`);
  console.log(`missingApiKey=${missingApiKey?.text}`);
  console.log(`preflight=${buildPreflightCompactionFailureText('Preflight compaction required but failed: proof reason', { includeDetails: true })}`);
  console.log('---');
}
NODE

git diff --check
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.test.ts extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.test.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.test.ts src/wizard/i18n/index.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

Evidence after fix: The runtime proof command printed English, zh-CN, and zh-TW output from the actual runtime translation and failure-formatting paths. Example output was:

```text
locale=en
generic=⚠️ Something went wrong while processing your request. Please try again, or use /new to start a fresh session.
missingApiKey=⚠️ Missing API key for OpenAI on the gateway. Use `openai/gpt-5.5` with the OpenAI OAuth profile, or set `OPENAI_API_KEY` for direct OpenAI API-key runs.
preflight=⚠️ Context is too large and auto-compaction could not recover this turn. Reason: proof reason. Try again, use /compact, or use /new to start a fresh session.
---
locale=zh-CN
generic=⚠️ 处理你的请求时出了问题。请重试，或使用 /new 开始一个新会话。
missingApiKey=⚠️ Gateway 缺少 OpenAI API Key。请使用带 OpenAI OAuth profile 的 `openai/gpt-5.5`，或为直接 OpenAI API Key 运行设置 `OPENAI_API_KEY`。
preflight=⚠️ 上下文过大，自动压缩未能恢复本轮。 原因：proof reason。 请重试，使用 /compact，或使用 /new 开始一个新会话。
---
locale=zh-TW
generic=⚠️ 處理你的請求時發生問題。請重試，或使用 /new 開始新工作階段。
missingApiKey=⚠️ Gateway 缺少 OpenAI API Key。請使用帶 OpenAI OAuth profile 的 `openai/gpt-5.5`，或為直接 OpenAI API Key 執行設定 `OPENAI_API_KEY`。
preflight=⚠️ 上下文過大，自動壓縮未能恢復本輪。 原因：proof reason。 請重試，使用 /compact，或使用 /new 開始新工作階段。
---
```

Observed result after fix: Default English output remains equivalent to the previous strings, zh-CN/zh-TW runtime output is localized, and the focused regression tests confirm localized failure strings still participate in silent-reply suppression, iMessage reflection detection, active-memory query cleanup, and QA-lab failure detection.

What was not tested: Full `pnpm check`, broad CI, live provider turns, and every command-specific text command surface were not run as part of this focused copy migration.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.test.ts extensions/active-memory/index.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/reply-failure.test.ts extensions/active-memory/index.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/reflection-guard.test.ts src/wizard/i18n/index.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings

target.title: fix(secrets): use configured default agent ID in auth/model path discovery (#90573)
target.body:
## Summary

- Replace hard-coded `"main"` with `resolveDefaultAgentId(config)` in the two storage-scan helpers that seed path discovery (`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs`, `listAgentModelsJsonPaths`).
- Drop the `agentId === "main"` early-exit branches. Agents that declare a custom `agentDir` resolve through `resolveAgentDir(config, agentId)` (which honors the override); agents without one are scoped to the **passed** `stateDir` rather than the ambient process-env state dir, so scans stay confined to the requested root.
- Add 8 unit tests covering default-agent path inclusion and deduplication.

## Motivation

Closes #90573.

`listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs()` and `listAgentModelsJsonPaths()` hard-coded `"main"` in two places each: the initial `paths.add()` seed and an `agentId === "main"` early-exit. In multi-agent deployments where the default agent is **not** `"main"` (e.g. `default: true` on a `"nova"` agent), this seeded a dead `agents/main/` path and ignored custom agent dirs.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** With a config whose default agent is `nova` (not `main`), `listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs` / `listAgentModelsJsonPaths` previously seeded `agents/main/agent` and routed configured agents through the ambient-env state dir, so the requested `stateDir` was not honored for non-default agents. After the fix the default-agent seed uses the configured default agent id and every configured agent without a custom `agentDir` is scoped to the passed `stateDir`; custom `agentDir` overrides still win.

**Real environment tested:** Ran the shipped `src/secrets/auth-store-paths.ts` and `src/secrets/storage-scan.ts` modules directly on this checkout (macOS arm64, Darwin 25.4.0, Node v25.5.0) via the repo's `tsx`, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state` and an isolated `HOME`. No mocks of the functions under test.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/oc-proof-state HOME=/tmp/oc-proof-home \
  npx tsx ./proof90603.mts
```

where `proof90603.mts` calls the two shipped exports with
`agents.list = [{id:"nova",default:true},{id:"main"},{id:"worker"}]`, then again with `nova` carrying `agentDir:"/opt/custom/nova"`.

**Evidence after fix (live stdout):**

```
== listAuthProfileStoreAgentDirs (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent
== listAgentModelsJsonPaths (nova default) ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/main/agent/models.json
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/worker/agent/models.json
== custom agentDir honored ==
  /tmp/oc-proof-state/agents/nova/agent
  /opt/custom/nova
```

**Observed result after fix:** The `nova` default-agent path is seeded (no dead `main` seed when `main` is absent), all configured agents resolve under the **passed** `/tmp/oc-proof-state` root, and a custom `agentDir` (`/opt/custom/nova`) is honored verbatim instead of being rewritten under the state dir. This matches the regression suite (`src/secrets/auth-store-paths.default-agent.test.ts`, 8/8 passing).

**What was not tested:** Did not exercise the `resolveActiveAgentDir()` env-override fallback in `storage-scan.ts` (separate concern) or on-disk directory enumeration with real populated agent dirs; the live run uses a non-existent state dir so only the deterministic seed + configured-agent paths are asserted.

target.title: fix: allow trusted host-read html after outbound staging
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Telegram/file outbound delivery for trusted generated `text/html` reports after the attachment is staged into the OpenClaw outbound media store.

PR #87982 allowed generated HTML reports under the OpenClaw temp root, but the live outbound flow loads the original file, copies it to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/<uuid>.html`, then the channel adapter loads that staged copy again. The second load no longer lives under the temp root, so host-read rejected it and the Telegram message was silently dropped.

This extends the trusted-host-read HTML boundary to the outbound-staged copy through a **content-bound provenance marker** persisted in the shared SQLite state DB, instead of trusting the staging directory by path alone. Trust is bound to the exact staged bytes and verified read-once at host-read time.

Fixes #90557.

## Design

When `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` stages a file whose source was a trusted-generated HTML path (under the temp root), it records a provenance row in a new `outbound_media_provenance` table keyed by the staged file's realpath, storing the `sha256` + `size_bytes` of the staged bytes (`kind = "trusted-generated-html"`, `version = 1`).

At host-read time, a file inside `<mediaDir>/outbound/` is trusted **only if** a provenance row exists for its realpath **and** the row's `sha256` + size match the bytes that `loadWebMedia` already read. Files under the temp root keep their existing unconditional trust. The provenance row is pruned in lockstep with the staged file by `pruneStaleTrustedGeneratedHtmlMarkers`, wired into the media-store retention pass (`cleanOldMedia`).

Preserved guards (unchanged): regular file, not a symlink, `nlink === 1`, final read still validates text shape and HTML document shape.

## ClawSweeper P1 resolution

Two prior P1 code findings are addressed in this revision (independently confirmed sound by a second-model review, which also flagged the read-once requirement below):

- **Path-keyed provenance bypass** → **content-bound + read-once.** The marker now stores `sha256` + `size_bytes`, and host-read trust is decided by hashing the **same buffer `loadWebMedia` already read** (no second filesystem read, so there is no hash-then-read TOCTOU window). A file whose bytes are replaced after the marker is written is rejected. New regression test: stage bytes `B1` + mark with the real digest, overwrite the same path with different bytes `B2`, assert host-read is rejected with `path-not-allowed`.
- **Swallowed marker write failure** → **propagate.** `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` no longer swallows `markTrustedGeneratedHtmlPath` errors; on failure it best-effort unlinks the staged file and rethrows, so an infrastructure/write error surfaces through the normal media-failure path instead of deferring to a later silent Telegram reload drop. New test asserts the rethrow and the best-effort `rm`.

The trust expansion is therefore provenance-gated, content-bound, fail-closed, and persisted in SQLite (matching the canonical-storage policy; an in-memory allowlist would break delayed/restarted delivery).

## Test plan

```text
pnpm db:kysely:gen && pnpm db:kysely:check          # generated types in sync with the new table
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/web-media.test.ts src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts
```

## Real behavior proof

### Deterministic real-code-path before/after (2026-06-24)

This exercises the **same production helpers the Telegram channel adapter uses** — `resolveOutboundAttachmentFromUrl` (stage) followed by `loadWebMedia(stagedPath, { hostReadCapability: true })` (the second reload) — on two worktrees that differ by **only this PR's diff**:

- **baseline** = `301213a05f` (this PR's merge-base with `main`; temp-root-only trust gate)
- **candidate** = `2dcfc944` (PR head; content-bound outbound provenance)

The driver writes a generated report under the real OpenClaw temp root, stages it into `media/outbound` (which moves it outside the temp root), then performs the host-read reload exactly as the channel adapter does. Environment: macOS (Darwin 25.4.0, arm64), Node v26.0.0.

```text
# baseline (301213a05f) — same source sha256 50fcd4b2…
[baseline] staged path   = …/media/outbound/pr90579-proof-…---<uuid>.html
[baseline] staged under media/outbound = true
[baseline] staged outside temp root    = true
[baseline] RESULT: REJECTED — staged reload host-read BLOCKED
[baseline]         LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads

# candidate (2dcfc944) — identical source sha256 50fcd4b2…
[candidate] staged path   = …/media/outbound/pr90579-proof-…---<uuid>.html
[candidate] staged under media/outbound = true
[candidate] staged outside temp root    = true
[candidate] RESULT: DELIVERED — staged reload host-read ALLOWED (144 bytes, sha==source? true)
[candidate] TAMPER CHECK: ✅ replaced bytes REJECTED — LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads
```

This is the exact production silent-drop (`LocalMediaAccessError: hostReadCapability permits only validated plain-text documents and trusted generated HTML reports for local reads`) on baseline, and delivery on candidate — with the live tamper step confirming the content binding rejects swapped bytes at the same staged path. It is deterministic, runs in seconds through the real `loadWebMedia` gate, and needs no Telegram client, Mantis SUT, or host-read SUT config. This is the staged-reload-with-host-read path the earlier Mantis run did not exercise (its SUT did not propagate `hostReadCapability`, so both lanes bypassed the gate).

### Original production trace + unit-level confirmation

Behavior addressed: when an agent reply emits `MEDIA:/tmp/openclaw/<file>.html`, OpenClaw's outbound pipeline loads the file once (`/tmp/openclaw/...` is inside the tmp root, so trusted), stages a copy to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/<file>---<uuid>.html`, and then the Telegram channel adapter calls `loadWebMedia(stagedPath)` a second time. Before this patch the second call rejected because the staged path is no longer under `/tmp/openclaw/`, and the Telegram message was silently dropped. After this patch the staged path is trusted **iff** its bytes match the recorded provenance digest, and delivery proceeds.

Real environment tested: live OpenClaw on macOS 26.4 (Darwin 25.4.0, arm64), Node v26.0.0, Telegram Bot API channel (polling). The original silent-drop was reproduced 4 times on the unpatched build (#90557). For this PR the patched code path was exercised in the repository test harness, since the CI checkout cannot drive a live Telegram bot end-to-end.

Evidence after fix (PR head):

```text
node scripts/generate-kysely-types.mjs --verify
# (exit 0 — generated Kysely types match the schema, incl. sha256 / size_bytes columns)

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/media/web-media.test.ts src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts
 ✓ media  src/media/web-media.test.ts (79 tests)
 ✓ media  src/media/outbound-attachment.test.ts (5 tests)
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  84 passed (84)
```

Key new coverage:

```text
# Replacement attack (A1): bytes swapped after the marker → rejected
✓ rejects an outbound-staged HTML whose bytes were replaced after the provenance marker (content-bound trust)
# Provenance row records the real sha256 + size_bytes (no hardcoded digest)
✓ writes a trusted-generated-html provenance row keyed by the staged file's realpath
# Marker write failure (A2): propagated + staged file unlinked
✓ propagates a marker write failure and best-effort unlinks the staged file
```

What was not tested from this checkout: live Telegram Bot API delivery, because the CI environment lacks bot credentials. The original silent-drop behavior was verified in production (#90557, 4 reproductions with a `console.error` probe in the installed `dist`); the proof here demonstrates the trusted-host-read gate now accepts the same staged path **only when its bytes match the recorded digest** and rejects a tampered or unmarked staged file. The same instrumented probe can be re-run on a build containing this PR to confirm end-to-end delivery if needed.

- Updates the Control UI browser tab title to include the active session name, for example `Release triage - OpenClaw Control`.
- Keeps the static `OpenClaw Control` title when no session is selected.
- Syncs the title when the active session changes or when refreshed session metadata updates labels/display names.

## Linked context

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?
None.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
No. This came from user feedback while working with multiple parallel OpenClaw sessions where every tab showed the same `OpenClaw Control` title.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: Browser tabs for different Control UI sessions were indistinguishable because every tab used the static `OpenClaw Control` document title.
- Real environment tested: Control UI unit/runtime helpers in the OpenClaw repo on Windows, using the UI Vitest project and production UI build.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```text
  node scripts\\run-vitest.mjs run ui\\src\\ui\\document-title.node.test.ts ui\\src\\ui\\app-lifecycle.node.test.ts
  ```

  ```text
  Tests 14 passed (14)

  ```
- Observed result after fix: The title resolver produces session-specific titles like `Release triage - OpenClaw Control`, and lifecycle sync updates `document.title` when `sessionKey` or `sessionsResult` changes.
- What was not tested: A manual browser screenshot was not captured.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This is a UI behavior change validated with targeted UI tests and production build rather than a live browser recording.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The static HTML title remains `<title>OpenClaw Control</title>`, and runtime code previously did not update `document.title` for the active session.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```text
node scripts\\run-vitest.mjs run ui\\src\\ui\\document-title.node.test.ts ui\\src\\ui\\app-lifecycle.node.test.ts
cd ui && npm run build
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added `document-title.node.test.ts` coverage for label, display name, fallback, and `document.title` sync behavior. Existing lifecycle tests were run to cover the integration point.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Parallel Control UI tabs all kept the static browser title `OpenClaw Control`, making sessions hard to distinguish.

If no test was added, why not?

A regression test was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
Yes. Browser tab titles now include the active session name.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)
No.

What is the highest-risk area?

The highest-risk area is ensuring the document title stays in sync with session metadata without affecting chat state persistence.

How is that risk mitigated?

The change is isolated to a document-title helper and lifecycle sync on `sessionKey`/`sessionsResult` changes, with targeted tests plus an unchanged passing production build.

Maintainer review and CI.

Waiting on maintainer review and CI.

None yet.

target.title: fix(agents): keep apostrophes in slash-command prompt-template arguments
target.body:
## Summary

- `parseCommandArgs` (`packages/agent-core/src/harness/prompt-template-arguments.ts`) expands `$ARGUMENTS` / `$@` / `$1..$N` in user prompt templates and custom slash commands. It does shell-style quote parsing with **no escape and no unbalanced-quote recovery**, so a lone `'` (an ordinary apostrophe in English prose: `user's`, `it's`, `don't`) opens a quote that runs to the next apostrophe (or end of string). The apostrophe is dropped and the following whitespace is swallowed into one token. The corrupted tokens then feed `$ARGUMENTS` (`args.join(" ")`) and the positional placeholders, so the model **silently receives a mangled instruction** with no error or warning.
  - `/cmd don't it's broken now` + a `[$1][$2]` template collapses to `ARG1=[dont its] ARG2=[broken]`: both apostrophes dropped, placeholders shifted.
- Reach: shipped and default-on. `AgentSession.prompt()` expands templates by default (`expandPromptTemplates ?? true`) and the **expanded text becomes the user message sent to the model**. Templates are real user `.md` files loaded by `loadPromptTemplates` from `<agentDir>/prompts`, `<cwd>/.openclaw/prompts`, and explicit prompt paths.
- Fix: single-quote handling becomes prose-aware while every other tokenization rule matches current main byte-for-byte. A word-attached apostrophe is a delimiter only when it opens a concatenation that closes at a later trailing apostrophe (`foo'bar baz'`), and it yields to any nearer apostrophe that owns such a closer, so a leading prose word keeps its apostrophe (`y'all foo'bar baz'`, `don't 'quoted text' next`). A standalone or assignment-attached apostrophe prefers a later whitespace-spanning quote (`'echo one two'`), otherwise closes at the first apostrophe that is not an English contraction suffix so shell-style concatenation still works before suffix text (`'foo'bar`, `'foo bar'baz` -> `foo barbaz`); with no closer at all it groups the rest of the input like the shipped parser (`say 'hello world` -> `["say", "hello world"]`) unless a later apostrophe could be prose it must not swallow, in which case it stays literal (`'review don't fail`). Irregular contractions are recognized structurally (no enumerated word list), so `y'all`, `o'clock`, `ne'er`, `cap'n`, and `y'know` are all preserved; only a small fixed set of English contraction suffixes (`d`, `ll`, `m`, `re`, `s`, `t`, `ve`) tells a contraction apostrophe from a closing quote that precedes suffix text. Double quotes and all non-apostrophe behavior are unchanged from main, including unmatched quotes (`say "hello world`). A full differential against the current-main parser confirms the only behavioral differences are the deliberate prose-apostrophe cases.
- One canonical change in `parseCommandArgs`; both consumers (`expandPromptTemplate` in `src/agents/sessions/prompt-templates.ts` and `formatPromptTemplateInvocation` in `packages/agent-core`) benefit with no API change. The separate `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.ts` `parseCommandArgs` (native channel commands) is a different function and is left untouched.

## Tests

- `packages/agent-core/src/harness/prompt-templates.test.ts`: added cases covering apostrophes preserved in parsed tokens and through `$ARGUMENTS` / positional placeholders; multiple contractions; shell-style quoted spans embedded in a token (`--title="two words"`, `foo='bar baz'`); bare embedded single-quote concatenation (`foo'bar baz'`); a multi-word quoted span that closes before suffix text (`'foo bar'baz` -> `foo barbaz`); an unmatched double quote that groups to the end of input (`say "hello world`); an unmatched single quote with no later apostrophe that groups to the end of input (`say 'hello world`, `'lonely`, `--title='hi there`); a plural possessive next to a later quoted span (`fix users' 'quoted text'`); apostrophes after non-ASCII word characters (`José's don't fail`); an unmatched leading quote that must not close on a later contraction (`'review don't fail`); a contraction inside a balanced quoted phrase (`'it's a test'`); a contraction before a later standalone single-quoted span (`don't 'quoted text' next`); irregular contractions before a later attached single-quote span (`y'all`, `ma'am`, `o'clock`); and a generality case proving any prose contraction is preserved without a fixed word list (`ne'er`, `cap'n`, `y'know`, `fo'c'sle`, plus closer-side `'y'all here'`). The existing quoted/multiline and placeholder-safety cases are unchanged and green (25/25).
- `src/agents/sessions/prompt-templates.test.ts` (`expandPromptTemplate`) unchanged and green.
- Touched suites run locally with the repo runner (`prompt-templates.test.ts` 25/25); tsgo core, type-aware oxlint, and oxfmt clean on the touched files.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a user-authored custom slash command / prompt template invoked with ordinary prose containing an apostrophe silently lost the apostrophe (and, for positional `$N` templates, collapsed arguments and shifted later placeholders), so the model received a corrupted instruction. Covers prose contractions of every shape including irregular ones the parser does not enumerate (`y'all`, `o'clock`, `ne'er`, `cap'n`, `y'know`) and a contraction inside a balanced span (`'y'all here'`). Every non-apostrophe tokenization rule is verified byte-identical to current main: multi-word concatenation (`'foo bar'baz`), unmatched double quotes (`say "hello world`), and unmatched single quotes (`say 'hello world`).
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22.19, exercising the actual production functions `loadPromptTemplates` + `expandPromptTemplate` (the same expansion path `AgentSession.prompt()` uses to build the outbound user message) over a real on-disk prompt directory (`<agentDir>/prompts/*.md`).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote `<tmp>/prompts/cmd.md` = `ARG1=[$1] ARG2=[$2] ARG3=[$3] ARG4=[$4]`; loaded it via `loadPromptTemplates({ cwd, agentDir, promptPaths, includeDefaults: false })`; called `expandPromptTemplate("/cmd <input>", templates)` for each input below; ran the script over the **current PR head** (`9b0ff9836e`) and, for the before column, over the `origin/main` parser source (`substituteArgs` is identical, so only `parseCommandArgs` differs) with the rest of the path identical.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live output from running the real `expandPromptTemplate` over the on-disk template on the current PR head `9b0ff9836e`:

```
TEMPLATES LOADED: ["cmd"]
USER TYPES : /cmd don't it's broken now
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[don't] ARG2=[it's] ARG3=[broken] ARG4=[now]

USER TYPES : /cmd say "hello world
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[say] ARG2=[hello world] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd say 'hello world
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[say] ARG2=[hello world] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd 'foo bar'baz next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[foo barbaz] ARG2=[next] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd José's don't fail
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[José's] ARG2=[don't] ARG3=[fail] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd 'review don't fail
MODEL GETS : ARG1=['review] ARG2=[don't] ARG3=[fail] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd 'it's a test' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[it's a test] ARG2=[next] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd don't 'quoted text' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[don't] ARG2=[quoted text] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd y'all foo'bar baz' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[y'all] ARG2=[foobar baz] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd o'clock foo'bar baz' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[o'clock] ARG2=[foobar baz] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd ne'er foo'bar baz' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[ne'er] ARG2=[foobar baz] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd cap'n foo'bar baz' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[cap'n] ARG2=[foobar baz] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd y'know foo'bar baz' next
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[y'know] ARG2=[foobar baz] ARG3=[next] ARG4=[]

USER TYPES : /cmd 'y'all here'
MODEL GETS : ARG1=[y'all here] ARG2=[] ARG3=[] ARG4=[]
```

- Observed result after fix: apostrophes survive end to end for prose contractions of every shape with no contraction word list in the parser, while shipped quote behavior is preserved: a multi-word quoted span before suffix text concatenates (`'foo bar'baz` -> `foo barbaz`), an unmatched double quote groups to the end of input (`say "hello world`), and an unmatched single quote with no later apostrophe groups to the end of input (`say 'hello world`). An unmatched leading quote before a contraction stays literal (`'review don't fail`). The model receives the exact instruction the user typed.
- What was not tested: the separate native-channel `src/auto-reply/commands-registry.ts` `parseCommandArgs` (a different function, untouched); a fully wired gateway round-trip through a live provider (the proof drives the exact expansion function that produces the user message rather than issuing a model call).
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: none specific; the expansion is deterministic and reproduced on demand.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the same script over the `origin/main` parser (unpatched) corrupts every prose case; the preserved forms (`'foo bar'baz`, `say "hello world`, `say 'hello world`) are identical before and after, confirming compatibility:


target.title: fix(memory-core): guard searches during safe reindex
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes an intermittent memory search/status race where safe full reindex temporarily points the manager at a half-built temp DB, allowing readers to see chunks without matching index metadata and fail closed with `index metadata is missing`.
- Adds a reindex latch so search waits for in-flight full reindex work before reading index identity, FTS availability, or query state.
- Keeps status reads stable during the temp-DB window by serving the last durable status snapshot.
- Rewrites the verified metadata on the canonical DB after the safe swap so the serialized-meta cache from the temp DB cannot skip the durable write.

Why does this matter now?

- #90361 reports intermittent built-in memory search failures even though the durable index is valid. The failure mode is timing-dependent, so a focused race guard is needed rather than only metadata recovery.

What is the intended outcome?

- Searches and status reads no longer observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex.
- Immediate post-reindex reads see valid index identity metadata on the canonical DB.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Remote embedding provider stress testing.
- Broader recovery for previously corrupted or stale on-disk memory stores.
- Changes to memory config, provider selection, or migration behavior.

What does success look like?

- Forced reindex, status, and search on a real CLI setup complete without `index metadata is missing`.
- Regression tests cover concurrent status/search reads, the provider-initialization await gap, and a reader that is already active when full reindex reaches the DB swap.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the reindex latch is drained before every search path that reads index identity, FTS availability, or query data.
- Whether the stable status snapshot is limited to the safe reindex temp-DB window.
- Whether the post-swap metadata rewrite is idempotent and scoped to full safe reindex.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90361

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #90422
Related #90395

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No direct maintainer request; this is a contributor fix for #90361.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: built-in memory search/status should not fail with `index metadata is missing` after a safe forced reindex when the durable OpenClaw index is valid.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout at `0a9c4d1ef5`, Node `v24.15.0`, real OpenClaw CLI memory commands, temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, temporary workspace, FTS-only memory provider (`provider: "none"`, `model: "fts-only"`) to avoid external credentials.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
export OPENCLAW_HOME=<tmp-home>
export OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<tmp-config>/openclaw.json
export OPENCLAW_BUILD_ALL_NO_PNPM=1
export OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1
export CI=true
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory index --force --agent main --verbose
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory status --deep --index --agent main --json
node scripts/run-node.mjs memory search --agent main --query 'durable index identity' --max-results 5 --json
```

Temporary config used for the proof:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "workspace": "<tmp-workspace>",
      "memorySearch": {
        "provider": "none",
        "model": "fts-only",
        "cache": { "enabled": false },
        "sync": { "watch": false, "onSessionStart": false, "onSearch": false }
      }
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "default": true }]
  }
}
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
## Real OpenClaw CLI proof for #90361
repo_commit=0a9c4d1ef5
node=v24.15.0

### Force rebuild index
Memory Index (main)
Provider: none (requested: none)
Model: fts-only
Sources: memory (MEMORY.md + <tmp-path>)

[memory] sync: indexing memory files
Memory index updated (main).

### Parsed proof summary
status_files=1
status_chunks=1
status_dirty=false
status_index_identity=valid
status_has_missing_metadata=false
search_result_count=1
search_has_missing_metadata=false
search_contains_durable=true
search_first_path=MEMORY.md
search_first_snippet="Alpha topic about durable index identity.\n\nThis note proves memory search can recall the durable OpenClaw index after safe reindex.\n"
```

- Observed result after fix: forced CLI reindex completed, status reported a valid index identity with two indexed chunks, and CLI search returned a `MEMORY.md` result. Neither status nor search output contained `index metadata is missing`.
- What was not tested: live gateway tool invocation, remote/OpenAI embedding provider, concurrent stress across separate OS processes, cross-OS proof.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the real behavior proof uses the real CLI and SQLite/FTS memory path, but intentionally uses FTS-only provider mode so no external API key or embedding service is required. Provider/vector concurrency is covered by focused regression tests, not by live remote-provider proof.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug is timing-dependent; this PR adds regression coverage that forces searches/status reads into the safe reindex temp-DB window and the provider-initialization await window.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts
pnpm exec oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-ops.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
```

Real CLI proof commands are listed in the Real behavior proof section.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.reindex-read-race.test.ts`.
- Covers `status()` during in-flight full safe reindex.
- Covers `search()` waiting during the temp-DB window.
- Covers `search()` waiting when provider initialization yields and another actor starts a full safe reindex before identity/FTS reads.
- Covers full safe reindex waiting for an already-active index reader before swapping the DB.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The race allowed search/status readers to observe the half-built temp DB during safe reindex and fail closed with invalid or missing index identity metadata.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Memory search/status now wait through an in-flight full safe reindex instead of reading transient temp-DB state.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Search can now wait for an in-flight full reindex before reading index identity or FTS state.
- Full safe reindex writes verified metadata again after reopening the canonical DB.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The latch is refcounted and cleared in `finally`.
- Active index readers are counted while DB reads run, and safe full reindex waits for them before swapping/closing the canonical DB.
- Status uses only a captured stable snapshot while the safe reindex temp DB is active.
- The post-swap metadata rewrite uses the same `nextMeta` built and verified during the full reindex.
- Focused regression tests cover the search/status race windows.
- Real CLI proof covers forced reindex, status, and search after the patch.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Ready for maintainer review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI needs to run on the PR.
- Maintainers may request additional live remote-provider stress proof if they want coverage beyond the local CLI/FTS proof and focused regression tests.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Fresh local autoreview completed cleanly: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main` reported `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`.

target.title: refactor: add SQLite embedded run target adapter
target.body:
## What

Implements the SQLite adapter side of the embedded-agent run/session target seam. The adapter resolves storage-neutral run/session targets through SQLite-backed session access while retaining the named active artifact boundary needed by current embedded-run internals.

## Why

This is the 3.2 counterpart to the file-backed embedded run/session target seam. It proves the target contract can operate under SQLite wiring before the production storage flip.

## Changes

- Adds SQLite target resolution
- Persists active artifact identity
- Handles stale artifact recovery
- Covers consult fork scoping

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/run-session-target.test.ts src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts src/commitments/runtime.test.ts src/crestodian/assistant.test.ts src/hooks/llm-slug-generator.test.ts src/talk/agent-consult-runtime.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts`

- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base clawdbot-587/32-sqlite-store-foundation --no-web-search --thinking codex=low`

Refs #88838

target.title: feat(plugins): allowStateAccess permission for agent_end without conversation content
target.body:
Closes #74580

## What Problem This Solves

Non-bundled plugins that only need run-level outcome data from `agent_end` (success/failure, duration, runId) are forced to declare `allowConversationAccess: true` — a flag that also grants access to raw conversation content (`llm_input`, `llm_output`, `before_agent_finalize`). This is intimidating for users who just want a display to show when the agent is thinking.

PR #78242 by @hclsys attempted to fix this but was closed because `allowStateAccess` was only added to the registry gate — the config normalization pipeline in `config-normalization-shared.ts` silently dropped the field before it ever reached runtime policy checks.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add `hooks.allowStateAccess: true` as a granular, additive, non-breaking alternative. When set without `allowConversationAccess`, `agent_end` events are dispatched with `messages: []` — conversation content stays protected.

`allowConversationAccess=true` still implies state access, so existing configs are unaffected.

## User Impact

Plugin authors who only need agent lifecycle state (e.g. a display showing processing/sleeping) can now set `allowStateAccess: true` instead of the intimidating `allowConversationAccess: true`. Existing configs with `allowConversationAccess: true` continue working unchanged.

## Changes (17 files, +194/-9)

**Core:**
- `hook-types.ts` — `STATE_HOOK_NAMES = ["agent_end"]`, `isStateHookName()` predicate, moved `agent_end` out of `CONVERSATION_HOOK_NAMES`
- `registry.ts` — State hook gating: allowed if `allowStateAccess` OR `allowConversationAccess`. When only state access, handler is wrapped to strip `messages` → `[]`
- `config-normalization-shared.ts` — **Critical fix for #78242**: `allowStateAccess` preserved through normalization pipeline

**Config & Schema:**
- `zod-schema.ts` — `allowStateAccess: z.boolean().optional()`
- `types.plugins.ts` — Type + JSDoc
- `schema.help.ts` — Public docs for the new field
- `schema.labels.ts` — UI label

**Startup & Status:**
- `gateway-startup-plugin-ids.ts` — `allowStateAccess` triggers hook-policy startup
- `status.ts` — Included in plugin inspect report
- `compat/registry.ts` — Surface for compat checks

**Tests:**
- `config-misc.test.ts` — Schema acceptance/rejection
- `config-state.test.ts` — Normalization preservation + standalone state access
- `channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` — Startup intent with only `allowStateAccess`
- `schema.help.quality.test.ts` — Help key coverage for `allowStateAccess`
- `loader.test.ts` — Updated conversation hook block test (6 hooks), added state hook allow/block tests

**Changelog:** `CHANGELOG.md` — Unreleased entry

## Security

- `messages` field in `agent_end` events remains behind `allowConversationAccess` — state-only plugins receive `messages: []`
- No new dependencies, workflows, or package scripts
- Additive and non-breaking: `allowConversationAccess=true` implies state access

## Evidence

This is our feature request (#74580) from the OpenClaw Interaction Bridge plugin — a physical display that only needs agent lifecycle state. The plugin currently requires `allowConversationAccess: true` just to show sleeping/processing faces. With this change, it can set `allowStateAccess: true` instead.

**Test updates:**
- `loader.test.ts`: Split `agent_end` out of conversation hook block test (6 conversation hooks + 1 state hook with different diagnostic message). Added `allows state hooks when allowStateAccess is enabled` and `blocks state hooks when neither flag is set` tests.
- `schema.help.quality.test.ts`: Assert `agent_end` appears in `allowStateAccess` help text, NOT in `allowConversationAccess` help text.
- `config-state.test.ts`: Normalization preserves `allowStateAccess` through pipeline.
- `channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`: Startup intent recognizes `allowStateAccess`.

CI push ffe5c03 failed `checks-fast-bundled-protocol` due to `loader.test.ts` expecting `agent_end` in conversation hooks (7 blocked → 6 blocked). Fixed in push 15c2d48 with updated test assertions.

target.title: feat(codex): scope user MCP server attachment by the tool allowlist
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

An OpenClaw operator running a **Codex-runtime agent** cannot give that agent access to a configured MCP server without also granting it Codex's full native code-mode shell/filesystem surface. The two are conflated behind a single gate.

In `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`, `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` returns true only when the effective tool allowlist contains a literal `*`. That one boolean (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled`) is then reused, unchanged, in `attempt-startup.ts` as `userMcpServersEnabled` — which decides whether `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projects `cfg.mcp.servers` into the Codex thread at all. Consequences:

1. **All-or-nothing.** Any least-privilege allowlist (`tools.allow` / `tools.alsoAllow` without `*`) silently disables **every** user MCP server, even ones the allowlist explicitly names. The MCP process spawns but its tools never register in the compiled toolset. Operators must choose between a full wildcard (shell + every server) or a scoped list (no MCP at all).
2. **Over-exposure when servers do attach.** `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projected **every** enabled `cfg.mcp.servers` entry, filtered only by the per-server `codex.agents` field — it never consulted the tool allowlist. So a host with multiple servers exposes all of them together, including tools the operator never granted.

This is a general gap for anyone running a scoped Codex agent with any MCP server, not a single integration.

## What Changed

Decouple the two capabilities and make MCP attachment flow through the same allowlist operators already use for every other tool:

- **Native code mode is unchanged** — `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` keeps its fail-closed, wildcard-only rule. The dangerous, sandbox-sensitive surface is no less locked down.
- **`shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`** now answers only the orthogonal question *"is it safe to attach external MCP servers this turn?"* (not a memory-flush run; OpenClaw sandbox, if active, can be honored). The per-server decision moves to the projection.
- **`resolveCodexMcpServerAllow(serverName, toolsAllow)`** is the single owner of the allowlist→server rule over OpenClaw's `<server>__<tool>` tool naming:
  - `undefined` allowlist → include, all tools (legacy unrestricted behavior preserved)
  - `*` → include, all tools
  - `<server>__*` → include that server, all tools
  - `<server>__<tool>` → include that server, scoped to the named tool(s)
  - no matching token → exclude the server
- **`buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch`** consults the resolver and projects **only** the servers the allowlist references, closing the over-exposure. The thread-config fingerprint already varies with the patch contents, so an existing thread rebinds when the allowlist changes which servers are in scope.

Attachment is enforced at **server granularity**. Per-tool restriction *within* an attached server is intentionally out of scope here: Codex's thread config exposes no per-tool MCP filter today (only `default_tools_approval_mode`), so the reliable enforcement point is the `pre_tool_use` native hook relay — a focused follow-up. Operators can already use `codex.defaultToolsApprovalMode` and their server's own tool set to constrain within a server.

## Files

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts` — decoupled `shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts`, `attempt-startup.ts`, `thread-lifecycle.ts` — plumb `userMcpServersEnabled` separately and pass `toolsAllow` into the projection
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts` — `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`, `codexToolsAllowReferencesMcpServers`, allowlist-aware projection
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md` — documents allowlist-driven MCP attachment
- tests as below

## Evidence

Unit tests (run in the worktree against the repo toolchain, `vitest run`):

- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts` — **23 passed**. New cases: undefined/`*` attach all; `opik__*` attaches only opik (not a second configured `notion`); exact tool name `opik__list` attaches the server; `["message"]`/`[]` attach nothing; `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow` and `codexToolsAllowReferencesMcpServers` truth tables.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` — **51 passed**. New cases: native surface stays wildcard-only while MCP gate is independent; memory-flush attaches no MCP servers.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle*.test.ts` + `attempt-startup.test.ts` — **84 passed** (existing suites green with the new plumbing).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt*.test.ts` — **121 passed** (1 unrelated `/tmp` rmdir cleanup race, `ENOTEMPTY`, passes in isolation).

Type safety: the standalone `tsc -p extensions/codex/tsconfig.json` surfaces only **pre-existing** plugin-sdk `dist`-sync errors (CI rebuilds the SDK first); grepping its output for every symbol this PR adds/changes (`shouldEnableUserMcpServers`, `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`, `codexToolsAllowReferences`, `userMcpServersEnabled`) returns **no** errors — the diff introduces none.

## Security posture

- Native code-mode shell/file access still requires a literal `*` — unchanged.
- Default-open behavior for unrestricted agents (`undefined` allowlist) preserved.
- Sandbox honoring preserved: an active OpenClaw sandbox without the exec-server path still attaches no user MCP servers.
- Net effect is *tighter*, not looser: scoped allowlists now attach only the referenced servers instead of all of them.

## Follow-up

Per-tool deny within an attached server, enforced in the `pre_tool_use` native hook relay (the only layer that sees natively-attached MCP tool calls today).
